_meta.authoringNotes: Umbrella page covering both allergic (Type IV hypersensitivity) and irritant (innate barrier injury) contact dermatitis. Cross-references dedicated subpages /allergic-contact-dermatitis and /irritant-contact-dermatitis. Citations cross-verified against AAD 2014 contact dermatitis guideline (Bonchak/Yiannias context), American Contact Dermatitis Society 2024 core allergen series, ESCD/EADV consensus, North American Contact Dermatitis Group 2019-2020 patch test data (DeKoven Dermatitis 2023, PMID 36917520), Bonchak/Yiannias 2014 PMID 25234040, Johansen JAID 2022 PMID 35183605, Apfelbacher BJD 2025 hand eczema PMID 39797908, Diepgen Br J Dermatol occupational data.

h1Title: Contact Dermatitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Contact dermatitis is an inflammatory skin reaction that develops where the skin meets an offending substance, producing itch, redness, blistering, or scaling that traces the exact outline of the exposure. It is the most common occupational skin disease, accounting for roughly 80% of all work-related dermatologic claims, and 15-20% of the general population experiences a clinically significant episode at some point in life. The condition has two distinct mechanisms: irritant contact dermatitis, an innate-immune injury triggered by water, soaps, solvents, or friction, and allergic contact dermatitis, a delayed Type IV hypersensitivity to a specific chemical such as nickel, fragrance, hair dye, or poison ivy. Both forms look alike at the bedside, but the workup and long-term plan diverge sharply. Patch testing identifies the true allergen in the allergic subtype, while a careful exposure history defines the irritant subtype. Strict avoidance plus short courses of topical corticosteroids resolve the majority of cases within 2-4 weeks.

keyStats.prevalence: 15-20% of the general population experiences a clinically significant episode of contact dermatitis during their lifetime
keyStats.demographics: Women affected more than men due to cosmetic, jewellery, and wet-work exposures; nickel sensitization 17% in women vs 3% in men across industrialized countries (Ahlström CD 2019)
keyStats.avgAge: Can present at any age; occupational cases concentrated in the first 2 decades of working life (ages 20-40); cosmetic and metal cases peak ages 20-65
keyStats.globalCases: Accounts for 80% of all occupational skin disease globally; chronic hand eczema (the dominant chronic presentation) affects 5-9% of European adults at any time (Apfelbacher BJD 2025)

definition: Contact dermatitis (ICD-10: L25.9, unspecified contact dermatitis) is an inflammatory eczematous skin reaction caused by external contact with a substance that either injures the epidermal barrier directly or triggers a delayed cell-mediated immune response. It is divided into two principal forms with distinct pathophysiology. Irritant contact dermatitis (ICD-10: L24) is a non-immunologic reaction in which water, detergents, solvents, alkalis, acids, oils, or mechanical friction strip stratum corneum lipids, denature keratin proteins, and activate keratinocyte innate-immune signaling — releasing IL-1, TNF-α, and chemokines that recruit neutrophils. It is dose-dependent, occurs on first exposure, and affects anyone given enough contact. Allergic contact dermatitis (ICD-10: L23) is a delayed Type IV hypersensitivity in which T cells primed against a specific hapten produce an itchy vesicular rash 24-72 hours after re-exposure. Only previously sensitized individuals react, and even microgram exposures can elicit florid dermatitis. The condition is staged clinically into acute, subacute, and chronic phases based on morphology — vesicular and oozing in the acute phase, scaly and erythematous in the subacute phase, and lichenified, fissured, and hyperkeratotic in the chronic phase. Contact dermatitis sits inside the wider eczema family alongside atopic dermatitis, seborrheic dermatitis, and stasis dermatitis, and is managed primarily by dermatologists with allergists, occupational physicians, and primary care heavily involved.

typesClassification.description: Non-immunologic barrier injury accounting for roughly 80% of all contact dermatitis cases. Caused by water, soaps, detergents, solvents, alkalis, acids, friction, and low humidity. Dose- and time-dependent; anyone develops it with enough exposure. The leading cause of occupational hand dermatitis in wet-work professions (healthcare, food service, hairdressing, cleaning, construction).

Delayed Type IV cell-mediated hypersensitivity to a specific chemical hapten. Requires prior sensitization and produces an itchy vesicular eruption 24-72 hours after re-exposure. Top allergens: nickel, fragrance mix, methylisothiazolinone, neomycin, p-phenylenediamine, rubber accelerators, urushiol (poison ivy/oak/sumac).

Reaction restricted to sun-exposed skin from a substance that becomes irritating (phototoxic) or allergenic (photoallergic) when activated by UVA. Common triggers: NSAID gels (ketoprofen), sunscreens (oxybenzone), some fragrances, certain plants (psoralens in lime juice, celery, parsnip — phytophotodermatitis).

Immediate IgE-mediated wheal-and-flare reaction within 30 minutes of contact, distinct from the delayed eczematous reaction of ACD. Latex, raw foods (fish, shellfish, fruit), and ammonium persulfate in hair bleach are leading causes. Can progress to anaphylaxis in latex-allergic patients.

Generalized eczematous, dyshidrotic, or symmetric flexural eruption (baboon syndrome) after oral, intravenous, or inhaled exposure to an allergen in a previously sensitized patient. Reported with metals (nickel, gold), balsam of Peru in foods, antibiotics, and corticosteroids.

primarySymptoms: Intense itch over the contact area that develops within minutes for an irritant exposure or 24-72 hours after re-exposure for the allergic form, and frequently dominates the patient's experience over pain.
Erythema, edema, papules, and small clear vesicles in the acute phase that may coalesce into oozing, weeping plaques with overlying serous crust within the first few days.

Sharply demarcated geographic borders that follow the outline of exposure — a band where a watchband sits, a rectangle below the umbilicus from a nickel jeans stud, streaks along the forearm from a plant, palmar patches from rubber gloves.
Burning, stinging, or pain in heavily fissured areas, particularly on the hands, fingertips, lips, eyelids, and perianal skin where the stratum corneum is thin and constantly stretched.

Lichenification — thickened, leathery skin with exaggerated skin markings — over chronically exposed and scratched sites such as the dorsal hands, eyelids, ankles, and feet after weeks of ongoing contact.
Dryness, scaling, and painful fissuring in chronic irritant cases, especially on the fingertips and finger webs of wet-work professionals (nurses, hairdressers, food handlers, cleaners, mechanics).
Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation after the active dermatitis resolves, which can persist for months and is particularly prominent in skin of color.
Recurrent flares each time the patient re-encounters the offending substance, with shorter latency between exposure and eruption in allergic cases as immunologic memory deepens.

Eyelid swelling and erythema as an isolated presentation — characteristic of cosmetic, nail-product, and airborne allergic cases, since allergens transferred by the fingers concentrate on thin eyelid skin.

Spread of the eruption beyond the original contact site over days (autoeczematization or id reaction), producing scattered papulovesicles on the trunk and limbs even at sites the substance never touched.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent localized itch and faint redness under a new piece of jewellery, watchband, or belt buckle that improves when the item is removed
Increasing dryness and chapping of the dorsal hands within weeks of starting a wet-work job or using a new soap or detergent at home
Recurrent eyelid puffiness within 1-2 days of changing eye makeup, mascara, or facial moisturizer
Sharply outlined rash in an unusual distribution that maps to a specific exposure (wedding ring finger, perianal skin from wet wipes, lateral feet from rubber sandals)
Flares of a previously controlled eczematous patch whenever a specific cosmetic, topical medication, or workplace product is reintroduced
emergencySigns: Generalized erythroderma involving more than 90% of body surface with chills, fever, or dehydration — admit for fluid balance and systemic therapy
Spreading honey-coloured crusting, fever, or lymphangitic streaking suggesting Staphylococcus aureus superinfection or cellulitis requiring systemic antibiotics
Sudden painful clustered punched-out vesicles superimposed on the dermatitis — possible eczema herpeticum, a dermatologic emergency requiring IV acyclovir
Severe periorbital swelling restricting eye opening, photophobia, or visual change — same-day ophthalmology review to exclude secondary keratoconjunctivitis
Airway swelling, wheeze, or hypotension after contact with latex, raw food, or chemical agents — possible contact urticaria progressing to anaphylaxis, requiring intramuscular epinephrine

causes.description: Repeated wet-and-dry cycles strip stratum corneum lipids and damage keratinocytes. Wet work for more than 2 hours daily or more than 20 handwashes per day is the most common cause of occupational irritant hand dermatitis in nurses, food handlers, hairdressers, and cleaners. Friction from gloves compounds the injury.

Petroleum solvents, degreasers, cement (alkaline calcium hydroxide), and acid cleaners denature epidermal proteins on contact. Cement-induced dermatitis in construction workers is a classic chronic example, with the alkaline pH causing slow, often undetected, daily injury.

The most common contact allergen worldwide. Roughly 17-19% of women and 3% of men in the North American Contact Dermatitis Group 2019-2020 series reacted to nickel sulfate. Sources include jewellery, jeans buttons, belt buckles, watch backs, mobile phones, eyeglass frames, and surgical implants. The EU Nickel Directive has reduced regulated-product exposure but unregulated imports remain a problem.

Fragrance mix I and II detect 8-12% of patch-tested patients across NACDG and European registries. Hidden in cosmetics, hair care, household cleaners, and many products labeled 'unscented' that still contain masking perfumes. Balsam of Peru cross-reacts with cinnamates and benzoates in foods, occasionally triggering systemic contact dermatitis.

Used in liquid soaps, cosmetics, household cleaners, water-based paints, and industrial fluids. Reaction rates climbed from 1.4% in 2009 to over 13% in some European clinics by 2014 (Uter JEADV 2020) before regulatory tightening. Remain in the top 5 allergens globally.

PPD is one of the most potent contact sensitizers and the leading cause of severe scalp, face, and neck dermatitis in adults using permanent or semi-permanent dye. Black henna temporary tattoos contain extremely high PPD concentrations and can sensitize children within days, with lifelong consequences.

Thiurams, carbamates, and mercaptobenzothiazole are added during rubber manufacturing and present in latex and synthetic gloves alike. These cause delayed-type allergic hand dermatitis. Natural rubber latex protein causes a different, immediate IgE-mediated contact urticaria with anaphylaxis risk.

Neomycin and bacitracin are applied to broken skin, dramatically raising sensitization risk; NACDG positivity rates 7-9% each. Topical corticosteroid allergy is paradoxical but well-described, especially with tixocortol pivalate and budesonide markers.

causes.cause: Urushiol from poison ivy, oak, and sumac (allergic)
causes.description: Urushiol is an oily pentadecylcatechol found in the Toxicodendron plant family. It causes one of the most florid forms of acute ACD in North America, with linear streaks of vesicles erupting 24-48 hours after brushing against the plant. Up to 85% of the US population is sensitized.

riskFactors.factor: Wet-work occupation (healthcare, food service, hairdressing, cleaning, construction)
riskFactors.description: Wet hands for more than 2 hours daily or more than 20 handwashes per day at least doubles the risk of irritant hand dermatitis. Occupational contact dermatitis accounts for 80% of all work-related skin disease in industrialized countries.

riskFactors.factor: Pre-existing atopic dermatitis or filaggrin gene mutation
riskFactors.description: Atopic skin has a defective barrier and allows easier penetration of both irritants and allergens. Atopic patients have 2-3 times the risk of developing occupational hand dermatitis; filaggrin loss-of-function carriers have even higher rates.

Women have higher rates of cosmetic, fragrance, and metal-related ACD. Nickel sensitization runs 17% in women versus 3% in men across industrialized countries (Ahlström CD 2019), driven by cumulative jewellery and accessory exposure starting in adolescence.

riskFactors.factor: Repeated skin trauma, low humidity, or extremes of temperature
riskFactors.description: Friction, microabrasion, and cold dry air disrupt the stratum corneum and accelerate both irritant injury and percutaneous penetration of allergens. Winter hand dermatitis flares are largely driven by these factors.

riskFactors.factor: Frequent use of cosmetics, hair products, and topical medications
riskFactors.description: Each additional product is an additional sensitization opportunity. Patients using 10 or more personal-care products daily have markedly higher patch-test positivity rates than light users.

riskFactors.factor: History of leg ulcers or stasis dermatitis
riskFactors.description: Chronic application of topical antibiotics, fragrances, and lanolin to compromised skin makes leg-ulcer patients one of the highest-risk groups for ACD. Patch testing this population reveals positive reactions in over 60%.

Contain high-concentration PPD that can produce permanent sensitization within days, often in children and adolescents on holiday. Subsequent hair dye exposure causes severe ACD, and cross-reactivity with sulfonamide antibiotics and benzocaine can complicate medical care for life.
Active smoking is associated with a roughly 1.5-fold increased risk of chronic hand eczema in cohort data and worsens response to standard topical therapy.

affectedDemographics: Roughly 15-20% of the general population experiences a clinically significant episode of contact dermatitis during their lifetime
Occupational contact dermatitis is the most common occupational skin disease worldwide, accounting for 80% of work-related dermatologic claims
Nickel sensitization: 17-19% of women, 3% of men patch-tested in industrialized countries (NACDG 2019-2020, ESSCA)
Chronic hand eczema, the dominant chronic presentation, affects 5-9% of European adults at any given time (Apfelbacher BJD 2025)
Pediatric ACD prevalence is rising in parallel with adults, particularly driven by metal, MI preservative, and black-henna exposures
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis rests on a structured clinical history, a careful examination of the rash distribution, and selective use of patch testing to identify allergens. The history asks: where did the rash start, what was the patient touching with that body part, what does the work and home environment look like, and what cosmetics, medications, and hobbies are involved. Distribution often reveals the cause before any test is ordered — earlobes (nickel earrings), eyelids (cosmetics or nail product transferred by the hands), perianal (wet wipes, fragranced toilet paper), feet (rubber sandals, leather chromate), hands (occupational exposures, household products). Examination notes the acute morphology (vesicles, oozing, sharp borders) versus chronic morphology (lichenification, fissures, scale). The decisive test for the allergic subtype is patch testing, in which standardized allergens in petrolatum or aqueous vehicles are applied to the upper back under occlusive chambers for 48 hours and read at 48, 72-96 hours, and ideally a late reading at day 7 to capture late reactors such as neomycin, corticosteroids, and gold. Three reference panels dominate clinical use: the TRUE Test (36 allergens, FDA-cleared, ready-to-apply chamber tape), the North American 80 Series used by ACDS-affiliated clinics, and the European Baseline Series (currently 30 allergens). A positive reaction is read using ICDRG criteria as faint erythema, papules, vesicles, or bullae, and must be clinically relevant — meaning the patient is exposed to the allergen and the rash distribution fits. Irritant contact dermatitis is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion when patch testing is negative or shows only weak non-specific reactions, supported by a strong exposure history. Biopsy is rarely needed but shows spongiotic dermatitis indistinguishable from atopic eczema; histology helps mainly to exclude psoriasis, mycosis fungoides, or other mimics.

diagnosticTests.test: Patch testing with a standard baseline series
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for diagnosing allergic contact dermatitis. Identifies the specific chemical(s) the patient is sensitized to and guides definitive avoidance counseling. Sensitivity 70-80% for the leading allergens when an adequate panel is used.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: On day 1, allergens in small chambers are taped to the upper back. The patient keeps the area dry for 48 hours. Patches are removed and read at 48 hours, again at 72-96 hours, and ideally a late reading at day 7. The test does not involve needles or injections.

diagnosticTests.test: Extended patch test series (cosmetic, hairdresser, dental, metal)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Tests beyond the baseline panel when history points to a specific exposure category and the baseline series is negative or incompletely explanatory. The cosmetic, hairdresser, and dental series each add 30-60 additional allergens relevant to those exposures.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same application and reading procedure as the baseline series, with additional patches applied at the same visit. Total reading still completed over 7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Repeated open application test (ROAT) or use test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms clinical relevance of a positive patch test by applying the suspected product (cosmetic, moisturizer, medication) twice daily for 1-2 weeks to a small area of normal skin. Positive: dermatitis recurs at the test site.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient applies a pea-sized amount of the product to the inner forearm twice daily for up to 14 days and watches for redness, itch, or vesicles. Done at home, no clinic visit needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used to exclude alternative diagnoses such as psoriasis, mycosis fungoides, or autoimmune blistering disease when the dermatitis is atypical, refractory, or asymmetric. Histology of contact dermatitis shows spongiosis with lymphocytic and eosinophilic infiltrate.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 4 mm punch biopsy of involved skin under local anesthetic. The procedure takes 10 minutes and leaves a small stitch that is removed 7-14 days later.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when the dermatitis is weeping, crusted, or worsening despite topical steroids — Staphylococcus aureus superinfection is common and changes management.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A cotton swab is rubbed over the most inflamed or crusted area and sent to microbiology. Results return in 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when distribution suggests photo-allergic contact dermatitis (sun-exposed sites only). Duplicate sets of patches are applied; one set is irradiated with UVA after 24 hours and both are read together.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two patch test sessions and a brief UVA exposure during the second visit. Otherwise similar to standard patch testing.

treatmentOverview: Treatment runs on three parallel tracks that must all be addressed for durable control. The first and most important is identifying and avoiding the trigger. For allergic contact dermatitis, this means a definitive patch-test result followed by structured counseling on every product and material that contains the allergen, including hidden sources verified through services like the American Contact Dermatitis Society's Contact Allergen Management Program (CAMP) and the Mayo Clinic Contact Allergen Replacement Database (CARD). For irritant cases, it means reducing wet work, switching to fragrance-free and detergent-free cleansers, wearing protective gloves with cotton liners, and applying barrier creams. The second track is symptomatic anti-inflammatory therapy. Topical corticosteroids remain first-line and are selected by site and severity following the AAD 2014 contact dermatitis guideline — mild Class VI-VII steroids (hydrocortisone 1%, desonide 0.05%) on the face, eyelids, and intertriginous skin; mid-potency Class III-IV steroids (triamcinolone 0.1%, fluticasone 0.05%) on the trunk and limbs; and high-potency Class I-II steroids (clobetasol 0.05%, betamethasone dipropionate 0.05%) on thick skin such as palms, soles, and lichenified plaques, used for 2-4 weeks then tapered. Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1%, pimecrolimus 1%) are steroid-sparing alternatives, especially useful on the face and eyelids and for chronic maintenance. Oral antihistamines (cetirizine, hydroxyzine at night) reduce itch though they do not affect the underlying inflammation. The third track is severity-matched escalation. Severe acute flares — generalized eruptions, severe poison-ivy reactions, eyelid involvement disabling vision — are treated with systemic corticosteroids, typically prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day tapered over 14-21 days; shorter tapers reliably produce rebound. Chronic refractory cases benefit from phototherapy (narrow-band UVB or PUVA) and, in select hand-eczema patients, the oral retinoid alitretinoin or systemic immunomodulators (cyclosporine, methotrexate, azathioprine, dupilumab). Secondary infection is treated with mupirocin topically for limited disease or oral cephalexin or dicloxacillin for spreading impetiginization.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-potency topical corticosteroid (hydrocortisone 1%, desonide 0.05%)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for facial, eyelid, axillary, and groin dermatitis where higher-potency steroids cause atrophy, telangiectasia, and striae. Applied twice daily until clear, usually 1-2 weeks, then stopped or stepped down to a calcineurin inhibitor for maintenance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clears 70-80% of mild facial and intertriginous contact dermatitis within 2 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Mid-potency topical corticosteroid (triamcinolone 0.1%, fluticasone propionate 0.05%, mometasone 0.1%)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for trunk and limb dermatitis of moderate severity. Applied twice daily for 2-4 weeks then tapered. Limit continuous use on the trunk to 4 weeks to avoid atrophy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clearance in 70-85% of acute and subacute eczematous lesions within 3-4 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: High-potency topical corticosteroid (clobetasol propionate 0.05%, betamethasone dipropionate 0.05%)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for thick palmar, plantar, and lichenified skin and for severe acute flares on the trunk and limbs. Limit continuous use to 2 weeks per area; cycle on-and-off if longer therapy is required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in 60-75% of chronic hand and foot dermatitis when paired with allergen or irritant avoidance.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus 0.1% ointment, pimecrolimus 1% cream)
medicalTreatments.description: Steroid-sparing alternatives that do not cause skin atrophy, making them particularly useful on eyelids, face, neck, and intertriginous areas, and for long-term maintenance. Boxed warning about theoretical malignancy risk is not supported by long-term cohort data.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Roughly 60-70% improvement in facial and eyelid contact dermatitis at 6 weeks; comparable to mid-potency steroids on the face without atrophy risk.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg daily, hydroxyzine 25 mg at night)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce itch and improve sleep but do not affect the underlying T-cell- or innate-immune-driven inflammation. Sedating antihistamines at night can help break the itch-scratch cycle.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Subjective itch reduction in 50-60% of patients; objective skin improvement minimal.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day, tapered over 14-21 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe acute flares — extensive poison ivy, generalized ACD, eyelid involvement impairing vision. Tapers shorter than 2 weeks cause reliable rebound. Avoid in chronic maintenance because of long-term side-effect burden.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid resolution of severe acute flares in 80-90% of patients within 7-14 days when an adequate taper is used.

medicalTreatments.name: Phototherapy (narrow-band UVB, PUVA, or targeted UV)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for chronic refractory hand dermatitis or widespread dermatitis not responsive to topicals. Delivered 2-3 times weekly over 8-12 weeks in a hospital or clinic phototherapy unit.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically meaningful improvement in 50-70% of chronic hand-dermatitis patients at 12 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic immunomodulators (cyclosporine 3-5 mg/kg/day, methotrexate 10-25 mg weekly, azathioprine 1-3 mg/kg/day) and dupilumab
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe refractory cases not controlled by topical and phototherapy. Cyclosporine is the fastest-acting bridge; methotrexate is preferred for long-term maintenance. Dupilumab (300 mg subcutaneous every 2 weeks) is approved for atopic dermatitis and is increasingly used off-label for chronic refractory hand and contact eczema with encouraging case-series data.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-75% achieve adequate disease control with one of these agents when topical therapy has failed.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bland emollients and ceramide-containing moisturizers
alternativeTreatments.description: Apply 3-4 times daily to repair barrier function. Evidence-based core therapy for both irritant and allergic disease — barrier repair reduces flare frequency by roughly 30% in cohort studies of occupational hand dermatitis and is recommended in every major guideline.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cotton glove liners under occupational gloves
alternativeTreatments.description: Wearing thin cotton liners inside rubber, vinyl, or nitrile gloves absorbs sweat, reduces friction, and protects against accelerator allergens. Recommended in occupational dermatology guidelines and reduces glove-related hand dermatitis incidence in healthcare workers.

alternativeTreatments.name: Barrier creams with dimethicone or perfluoropolyether
alternativeTreatments.description: Applied before potential irritant exposure, these creams reduce penetration of water-soluble and lipid-soluble irritants. Modest effect in randomized trials — useful as an adjunct, not a substitute for avoidance.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should be involved when the dermatitis is widespread, recurrent, or fails to clear within 4-6 weeks of topical therapy, when patch testing is needed to identify a culprit allergen, when occupational disability is at stake, or when severe acute flares require systemic corticosteroids or immunomodulators. Occupational physicians become involved when workplace modification is required. Allergists collaborate when the differential includes contact urticaria or systemic allergic disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in dermatology with documented experience in patch testing — ideally membership in the American Contact Dermatitis Society (US), the European Society of Contact Dermatitis, or equivalent. Ask whether the practice offers an expanded patch test panel beyond the TRUE Test, late readings at day 7, and access to the Contact Allergen Management Program (CAMP) for personalised product avoidance lists. Continuity matters: contact dermatitis often requires repeat patch testing as exposures change.
preventionStrategies: Once an allergen is identified by patch testing, avoid every product containing it — verify each cosmetic, household, and workplace product against the CAMP or CARD database before use
Reduce wet work below 2 hours per day total and avoid hand washing more than 20 times daily; substitute alcohol-based hand rub for soap-and-water where infection control allows
Wear chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile for most chemicals; specialized gloves for solvents and cytotoxic agents) with cotton liners for wet-work and chemical exposures
Apply fragrance-free, dye-free moisturizer immediately after every hand wash and at least three times daily to maintain the skin barrier
Choose 'free-from' personal-care products labeled fragrance-free (not 'unscented'), formaldehyde-free, and methylisothiazolinone-free where exposures matter
Educate adolescents to avoid black henna temporary tattoos, which can cause permanent PPD sensitization within days
lifestyleModifications: Switch to fragrance-free laundry detergent and skip fabric softener — both are common hidden sources of fragrance and preservative exposure to skin
Replace nickel-containing jewellery with surgical-grade stainless steel, titanium, gold (14k or higher), or platinum; use a dimethylglyoxime spot test kit to check suspect items
Wear sun-protective clothing and broad-spectrum mineral sunscreens (zinc oxide, titanium dioxide) for patients with photo-allergic contact dermatitis to avoid oxybenzone and octocrylene
Keep a written list of confirmed allergens in your wallet or phone for use at salons, medical visits, and when shopping
Use lukewarm rather than hot water for showers and limit shower duration to under 10 minutes to reduce barrier damage
Choose cotton or silk over wool and synthetic fibers next to inflamed skin to reduce friction and irritation
Carry your own fragrance-free cleanser and moisturizer when travelling — hotel toiletries are a frequent flare trigger

recommended: Standard balanced diet — contact dermatitis is primarily a contact-driven condition, not a food-driven one
Adequate hydration to support epidermal barrier function (2 L water daily unless restricted)
Omega-3-rich foods (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) for general skin health and modest anti-inflammatory effect
Vitamin D-replete diet or modest supplementation (800-1000 IU daily) if deficient — vitamin D supports keratinocyte differentiation
Probiotic-rich foods (yogurt, kefir, fermented vegetables) — early data suggest a small role in barrier function
avoid: Foods rich in balsam of Peru cross-reactants (cinnamon, vanilla, cloves, citrus peel, tomato, chocolate) only for patients with confirmed systemic contact dermatitis to balsam of Peru
Foods naturally rich in nickel (cocoa, soy, oatmeal, whole grains, lentils, nuts) only for the small subset of nickel-allergic patients with confirmed systemic contact dermatitis from oral exposure
Alcohol-containing mouthwashes and toothpastes in patients with cinnamic aldehyde or fragrance allergy
Excessive caffeine if it worsens itch-related sleep disturbance
Unverified elimination diets — most contact dermatitis is not food-driven and rigorous elimination diets cause more harm than benefit

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is encouraged but pay attention to sweat-allergen interactions. For hand and foot dermatitis, dry hands and feet thoroughly after exercise, change out of damp gym clothes promptly, and rinse off chlorine after swimming. Patients with confirmed rubber accelerator allergy may need to switch to accelerator-free gym equipment grips and sports gear. Skin should be moisturized within 3 minutes of showering after workouts.
dailyManagement: Apply prescribed topical anti-inflammatory once or twice daily as directed; never share or use leftover steroids on other body sites without checking potency
Moisturize three or more times daily with a fragrance-free, dye-free product chosen from your CAMP-generated safe list
Carry a list of confirmed allergens (paper and phone) to show at salons, dental visits, and pharmacies
Inspect skin daily during a flare for crusting, weeping, or pain disproportionate to redness — early signs of secondary infection
Track flare triggers in a simple diary (location, product, environment) for the first 3-6 months after diagnosis to identify hidden exposures
Take prescribed antihistamine at bedtime if night-time scratching is breaking the skin
prognosis: Prognosis depends heavily on how completely the trigger can be avoided. When a single allergen is identified and removed — for example, a piece of nickel jewellery, a workplace product, or a topical medication — 70-80% of patients achieve full resolution within 4-6 weeks and remain clear long-term. When the allergen is occupational and exposure cannot be entirely eliminated despite reasonable accommodation, chronic disease persists in 30-50% of cases, particularly chronic hand eczema, which has the worst long-term trajectory: less than 50% of affected workers remain symptom-free at 10 years. For irritant contact dermatitis, complete avoidance of wet work and friction usually clears the rash within 2-4 weeks, but resumption of the same exposure reliably reproduces the dermatitis. Patients with concurrent atopic dermatitis or filaggrin mutations have a worse prognosis at every stage. Contact dermatitis is not life-threatening and does not progress to skin cancer, but it imposes substantial occupational and quality-of-life costs, with hand eczema being one of the leading causes of medical job change in industrialized countries.
recoveryTimeline: Acute contact dermatitis from a single exposure (such as poison ivy or a new cosmetic) typically peaks at 3-4 days and resolves over 2-3 weeks with topical or systemic corticosteroids. Subacute disease responds to topical therapy within 4-6 weeks once the trigger is removed. Chronic hand and foot dermatitis often requires 8-12 weeks of intensive topical therapy plus consistent avoidance and barrier care before substantial improvement appears, and full barrier recovery can take up to 6 months. Pigmentary changes (post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation) outlast the active dermatitis by 3-12 months.
complications: Chronic hand eczema with persistent fissuring, pain, and functional impairment — the leading cause of occupational disability from skin disease
Secondary bacterial infection (impetiginization) with Staphylococcus aureus, presenting as honey-coloured crusting, oozing, and fever
Eczema herpeticum — disseminated herpes simplex infection on the dermatitic skin, presenting as painful clustered punched-out vesicles and requiring urgent IV acyclovir
Generalized erythroderma from severe untreated or polysensitized allergic contact dermatitis, with risk of dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, and high-output heart failure
Post-inflammatory hyper- or hypopigmentation, especially in skin of color, that can persist for months after the dermatitis itself has resolved
Iatrogenic skin atrophy, striae, and telangiectasia from inappropriately prolonged or potent topical corticosteroid use
Occupational job loss and income loss when chronic hand dermatitis prevents return to wet-work or high-exposure occupations

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Contact Dermatitis Patient Information
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that writes the contact dermatitis guideline.

supportResources.name: American Contact Dermatitis Society — CAMP Program
supportResources.description: Patient-facing access to the Contact Allergen Management Program (CAMP), which generates personalised safe-product lists based on your patch test results.

supportResources.name: DermNet — Contact Dermatitis
supportResources.description: Plain-language clinical resource curated by New Zealand dermatologists with extensive image library.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Contact Dermatitis Patient Information
supportResources.description: Peer-reviewed UK patient leaflet covering diagnosis, patch testing, and management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Atopic dermatitis is a constitutional, chronic, relapsing eczema that begins in childhood and is associated with personal or family atopy (asthma, hay fever, food allergy). It favors flexural surfaces (antecubital and popliteal fossae, neck) and lacks the sharp geographic borders of contact dermatitis. Both can coexist — atopic skin is more prone to contact allergy and irritant injury, and patch testing is recommended when adult atopic dermatitis fails standard therapy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis produces well-demarcated salmon-pink plaques with silvery scale on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, scalp, lower back), with nail pitting and joint involvement in some patients. It is not itchy in the same way as contact dermatitis, lacks vesicles, and does not improve with allergen avoidance. Palmar psoriasis can mimic chronic hand contact dermatitis — biopsy and absence of patch-test reactions distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Seborrheic dermatitis produces greasy yellow scale in sebum-rich zones (scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, central chest) and is driven by Malassezia yeast colonization, not exposure. Itch is mild compared to contact dermatitis, there are no vesicles, and treatment is antifungal-based rather than allergen-avoidance based.

confusedWithConditions.name: Urticaria (Hives)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Urticaria presents as transient, migratory, wheal-like swellings that appear within minutes, last under 24 hours at any given site, and resolve without scaling. Contact dermatitis produces persistent inflammation that lasts days to weeks and scales as it heals. Contact urticaria (an immediate IgE-mediated reaction) is a separate entity that can coexist with allergic contact dermatitis to the same substance, especially latex.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rosacea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rosacea produces persistent central facial erythema, telangiectasias, and inflammatory papulopustules over the cheeks, nose, chin, and forehead, often triggered by alcohol, heat, and spicy food. It lacks the vesicles, oozing, and sharp borders of facial contact dermatitis. The two can coexist, and patch testing should be considered when rosacea fails standard topical therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The Type IV hypersensitivity subtype of contact dermatitis; diagnosed by patch testing and managed primarily by allergen avoidance.

relatedConditions.name: Occupational Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Most occupational dermatitis is contact dermatitis (irritant or allergic) acquired through workplace exposure; requires workplace accommodation and may qualify for compensation.

relatedConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
relatedConditions.relevance: Atopic patients have 2-3 times the risk of developing irritant and allergic contact dermatitis and often present with combined eczemas requiring patch testing.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema (Dermatitis)
relatedConditions.relevance: Contact dermatitis is one of the major subtypes within the broader eczema family; distinguishing the subtype changes the workup and management plan.

relatedConditions.name: Urticaria (Hives)
relatedConditions.relevance: Contact urticaria — an immediate IgE-mediated reaction to substances such as latex, raw foods, and persulfates — is distinct from delayed contact dermatitis but the two can coexist in the same patient.

faqs.question: What's the difference between allergic and irritant contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Allergic contact dermatitis is a delayed Type IV immune reaction to a chemical (nickel, fragrance, poison ivy) requiring prior sensitization. Irritant contact dermatitis is direct barrier injury from water, soaps, or solvents and is dose-dependent. Both look alike but workup and management differ.

faqs.question: How long does contact dermatitis take to heal?
faqs.answer: Acute contact dermatitis peaks at 3-4 days after exposure and resolves over 2-3 weeks once the trigger is removed and topical steroids started. Chronic hand and foot dermatitis often takes 8-12 weeks of treatment plus consistent avoidance before substantial improvement appears.

faqs.question: What does contact dermatitis look like?
faqs.answer: Acute contact dermatitis is red, swollen, itchy skin with tiny clear blisters that may ooze, often with sharp borders matching the area that touched the trigger. Chronic disease produces thick, leathery, scaly, fissured plaques most often on hands, feet, eyelids, and face.

faqs.question: Is contact dermatitis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Contact dermatitis is an inflammatory reaction, not an infection, and cannot spread from person to person. Even poison ivy is only transferable via residual urushiol oil on skin, clothing, or pet fur — not from blister fluid. Once the trigger is washed off, transmission is impossible.

faqs.question: How is contact dermatitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines exposure history with examination of rash distribution. For suspected allergic cases, patch testing with a panel of 36-80 allergens is the gold standard: patches stay on the back for 48 hours and are read at 48 hours, 72-96 hours, and day 7. Irritant cases are clinical diagnoses.

faqs.question: What is patch testing?
faqs.answer: Patch testing applies standardized allergens in small chambers to the upper back for 48 hours, with readings at 48 hours, 72-96 hours, and day 7 to identify chemicals the patient is allergic to. It involves no needles or medication and is the gold standard for allergic contact dermatitis.

faqs.question: What are the most common contact dermatitis allergens?
faqs.answer: The leading allergens worldwide are nickel, fragrance mix, methylisothiazolinone (MI) preservative, neomycin and bacitracin, p-phenylenediamine (PPD) hair dye, rubber accelerators, and urushiol from poison ivy, oak, and sumac. Together these account for the majority of positive patch tests.

faqs.question: Can contact dermatitis go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Mild cases often improve within 1-3 weeks once the trigger is removed, especially with cool compresses and bland moisturizers. Most clinically significant cases resolve faster with prescribed topical corticosteroids. Without removing the trigger, the rash recurs with each re-exposure.

faqs.question: What treatments work for contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Avoid the trigger and use topical corticosteroids matched to site and severity — low-potency on the face, mid-potency on the trunk, high-potency on palms and soles — usually for 2-4 weeks. Calcineurin inhibitors are alternatives for the face. Severe flares may need a 2-3 week oral prednisone taper.

faqs.question: Can I get contact dermatitis from soap?
faqs.answer: Yes. Harsh soaps, antibacterial cleansers, and frequent handwashing strip skin lipids and cause irritant contact dermatitis on the hands. Fragrance and preservatives in soap can also cause allergic reactions. Fragrance-free syndet cleansers and moisturizing after each wash usually resolve the rash.

faqs.question: Is contact dermatitis dangerous?
faqs.answer: Contact dermatitis is rarely life-threatening, but severe cases can cause skin infections, generalized erythroderma, and disability. Eczema herpeticum and anaphylaxis from latex contact urticaria are medical emergencies. Chronic hand eczema is a leading cause of occupational disability.

faqs.question: Can children get contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Yes — pediatric contact dermatitis is increasingly common, with metal (nickel, cobalt) and methylisothiazolinone allergies the leading triggers. Black henna temporary tattoos can permanently sensitize a child within days due to high PPD content. Patch testing is safe in children over age 2.

faqs.question: How do I find out what I'm allergic to?
faqs.answer: Patch testing performed by a dermatologist with contact allergy experience is the gold standard. A panel of 36-80 allergens stays on the back 48 hours, with readings at 48 hours, 72-96 hours, and day 7. Once identified, the CAMP database generates a personalised safe-product list.

faqs.question: Can contact dermatitis be cured?
faqs.answer: Allergic contact dermatitis cannot be cured — immune memory persists for life — but it can be completely controlled by avoiding the identified allergen. Irritant contact dermatitis fully resolves when the exposure stops and the barrier recovers. Both recur with re-exposure.

faqs.question: What is the strongest cream for contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% and betamethasone dipropionate 0.05% are the strongest topical steroids for thick skin on palms, soles, and lichenified plaques, used for up to 2 weeks. They are not appropriate for the face, eyelids, or skin folds. Always use under medical supervision.

faqs.question: Why does the rash come back when I stop my cream?
faqs.answer: Recurrence after stopping a topical steroid usually means the trigger has not been removed. The cream suppresses inflammation but does not change underlying sensitization or barrier vulnerability. Identify the trigger by patch testing or exposure review and remove it — otherwise the rash returns.

faqs.question: Can I still wear jewellery if I have nickel allergy?
faqs.answer: Yes — switch to surgical-grade stainless steel, titanium, gold of 14 karat or higher, platinum, or sterling silver verified nickel-free. A dimethylglyoxime spot test kit costs under USD 20 and detects nickel release. Coating cheap jewellery with clear nail polish is only a temporary fix.

faqs.question: Is contact dermatitis the same as eczema?
faqs.answer: Contact dermatitis is one major subtype within the broader eczema family. Eczema is often used loosely to mean atopic dermatitis, but technically contact dermatitis, atopic dermatitis, seborrheic dermatitis, and stasis dermatitis all belong under the eczema umbrella with different workups.

faqs.question: Can I get contact dermatitis from cosmetics?
faqs.answer: Yes — cosmetics are a leading cause of allergic contact dermatitis on the face and eyelids. Fragrances, methylisothiazolinone, formaldehyde-releasing preservatives, PPD in hair dye, and some sunscreen filters are top culprits. Hypoallergenic labels are unregulated; patch testing finds the trigger.

faqs.question: How do I know if my contact dermatitis is from work?
faqs.answer: Workplace contact dermatitis typically improves on weekends and holidays and flares within hours to days of returning to work. The rash usually starts on the hands or forearms and follows the exposure pattern. A dermatologist confirms with patch testing using a workplace-specific allergen series.

faqs.question: Does contact dermatitis go away with age?
faqs.answer: No — allergic sensitization is lifelong, so re-exposure to the chemical triggers a reaction at any age. Patients who learn their triggers and avoid them may appear to outgrow it, but the immune memory persists. Irritant dermatitis resolves with reduced exposure but recurs with re-exposure.

faqs.question: When should I see a dermatologist for contact dermatitis?
faqs.answer: See a dermatologist if the rash is widespread, recurrent, persists beyond 4-6 weeks of self-care, involves the face or genitals, interferes with work, or needs repeated oral steroids. A dermatologist performs patch testing, prescribes stronger therapy, and coordinates occupational accommodation.

simpleName: Contact Dermatitis (skin rash from touching something)
metaDescription: Contact dermatitis: itchy red rash where the skin met something irritating or allergic. Patch testing, treatment, avoidance, and prognosis from our board.

images.altText: Linear streaks of erythema and vesicles on the forearm characteristic of acute allergic contact dermatitis from poison oak urushiol exposure
images.caption: Acute allergic contact dermatitis from poison oak — linear streaks where the plant brushed the skin.

images.altText: Sharply demarcated erythematous patch on the wrist matching the outline of a watch strap, typical of nickel allergic contact dermatitis
images.caption: Allergic contact dermatitis to nickel — note the sharp geographic border matching the watch strap.

images.altText: Patch test chambers applied in rows to the upper back to identify contact allergens
images.caption: Patch testing — standardized allergens applied to the upper back and read at 48 hours, 96 hours, and day 7.

images.altText: Chronic hand contact dermatitis with thickened skin, scaling, and painful fissures on the fingers of a wet-work professional
images.caption: Chronic hand contact dermatitis — fissuring and lichenification in a wet-work professional.
